text: The salt marsh at low tide is mostly the color of old pewter, a flat expanse of mud veined with channels that the receding water has left behind, each one glistening like something freshly cut. Cordgrass stands in stiff, yellowing ranks, and the whole place smells of sulfur and brine, a rich rotten green smell that you stop noticing after a few minutes. Fiddler crabs come and go from their burrows in nervous little crowds, one oversized claw held up like a man checking his watch, and a heron stands so still at the edge of a creek that you mistake it for a stake until it moves. Out past the grass the land gives way by degrees to the sound, and the horizon is so low and level that the sky takes up almost everything, doing all the work the landscape refuses to do.
